title: Inference & Latency
eli8:
# Why Does AI Sometimes Feel Slow? ⚡

When you ask an AI a question, it doesn't think of the whole answer at once and then type it — it types **one word at a time**, as fast as it can!

## Two Phases of Answering 🔄

Think of the AI answering like a chef at a restaurant:

**Phase 1: Reading Your Order (Prefill) 📖**
- The AI reads your entire question at once
- This is fast — it can read all words in parallel!
- Like the chef reading the full order ticket at once

**Phase 2: Cooking the Food (Decoding) 🍳**
- Now the AI generates each word one by one
- Each word takes a little time
- Like cooking — you have to finish one dish before starting the next!

This is why you see that blinking cursor — the AI is generating tokens in real time!

## Measuring How Fast 📊

We measure AI speed in a few ways:

- **TTFT** (Time To First Token): How long until the first word appears? 🕐
- **TBT** (Time Between Tokens): How quickly does each next word come? ⏱️
- **Throughput**: How many words per second total? 💨

A fast AI might produce **50-100 tokens per second** — faster than you can read!

## Why Does Bigger = Slower? 🐘

Bigger models have more "brain cells" (parameters), so they:
- Take more memory (need bigger GPUs!)
- Do more math per token
- Are slower but smarter

Running a tiny 7B model is like driving a sports car ⚡
Running a giant 405B model is like driving a bus 🚌 — more capacity, but slower!

Companies use tricks like **quantization** (making the numbers smaller) to make big models faster and cheaper to run.
detail:
# Inference & Latency

## The Two Phases of LLM Inference

LLM inference has two distinct computational phases with very different characteristics:

### Phase 1: Prefill
- All input tokens are processed **in parallel** in a single forward pass
- Computes Q, K, V for every input token simultaneously
- GPU-compute-bound: can leverage full matrix multiplication parallelism
- Time scales roughly linearly with input length (but with efficient batching)
- Produces the KV cache for all input tokens

### Phase 2: Decode (Autoregressive Generation)
- Generates tokens **one at a time** — each token depends on all previous tokens
- Each decode step is a single forward pass computing one new token
- **Memory-bandwidth-bound**: the bottleneck is loading model weights from GPU HBM (High Bandwidth Memory), not computation
- Time scales with output length × latency-per-token

```
Total inference time ≈ prefill_time + num_output_tokens × time_per_token
```

## Key Latency Metrics

**TTFT (Time to First Token)**: Time from request submission to the first token appearing in the response. Dominated by prefill time for long prompts.
- Good TTFT: < 500ms for interactive applications
- Cloud APIs: typically 200–2000ms depending on load and context length

**TBT (Time Between Tokens)**: Time between successive output tokens during decode phase. Determines how fast text streams to the user.
- Target for smooth streaming: < 50ms TBT (> 20 tokens/sec)
- Typical range: 10–100ms per token

**Throughput**: Total tokens generated per second across all concurrent requests. Measured as tokens/sec (generation throughput) or requests/sec.

**End-to-end latency**: Total time from request to complete response = TTFT + (output_tokens × TBT)

## Why Decode is Memory-Bandwidth-Bound

During decode, each step generates only 1 new token — a tiny matrix-vector multiply rather than a large matrix-matrix multiply. The GPU computation finishes quickly, but must wait for model weights to be loaded from HBM:

- NVIDIA A100 HBM bandwidth: ~2 TB/s
- LLaMA 3 70B in FP16: ~140 GB weights
- Minimum load time: 140 GB / 2 TB/s = 70ms per token → ~14 tokens/sec
- Batching 8 requests: same bandwidth, 8× throughput = ~112 tokens/sec

This is why **batching** is the primary throughput optimization for decode: amortize the weight-loading cost across multiple concurrent requests.

## Quantization and Its Latency Impact

Quantization reduces the precision of model weights, shrinking memory footprint and reducing bandwidth requirements:

| Precision | Bits/param | LLaMA 3 70B size | Approx. tokens/sec (A100) |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| BF16/FP16 | 16 bits | ~140 GB | ~14 |
| INT8 | 8 bits | ~70 GB | ~25–30 |
| INT4/GPTQ | 4 bits | ~35 GB | ~45–60 |
| 2-bit | 2 bits | ~18 GB | experimental |

Modern quantization methods (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF) maintain most model quality at INT4 precision.

## vLLM and PagedAttention

**vLLM** (Kwon et al., 2023) is an open-source inference server that achieves high throughput through **PagedAttention** — a KV cache memory management system inspired by OS virtual memory:

- Divides KV cache into fixed-size "pages" that can be allocated/freed dynamically
- Enables near-zero memory waste (standard systems waste 60–80% of KV cache memory due to fragmentation)
- Enables continuous batching: new requests join a running batch as soon as capacity frees up
- Achieves 2–24× higher throughput than naive HuggingFace inference

## Hardware: GPU HBM as the Bottleneck

For LLM inference, **GPU HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)** is the critical resource:

- **Capacity**: Determines max model size and KV cache at a given batch size
  - A100 80GB: fits LLaMA 3 8B (FP16) with large KV cache, or 70B with quantization
  - H100 80GB: similar capacity but 2× memory bandwidth
- **Bandwidth**: Determines decode speed (weights must be loaded each token)
- Multiple GPUs needed for large models (tensor parallelism)

## Cost: $/1M Tokens

Cloud LLM pricing (approximate, July 2025):

| Model | Input ($/1M tokens) | Output ($/1M tokens) |
|-------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| GPT-4o | $5 | $15 |
| GPT-4o-mini | $0.15 | $0.60 |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | $3 | $15 |
| Claude 3 Haiku | $0.25 | $1.25 |
| Gemini 1.5 Flash | $0.075 | $0.30 |

Output tokens cost more than input tokens because each output token requires a separate forward pass during decode, while input tokens are processed in parallel.

## Latency Budget for Production Apps

- **Interactive chat**: Total latency < 3s, TTFT < 500ms
- **Autocomplete / copilot**: TTFT < 100ms, TBT < 30ms
- **Batch summarization**: Total throughput matters, individual latency is secondary
- **Agentic pipelines**: Latency multiplies across chained calls — each hop adds cost

mcqs.question: What is the difference between the prefill and decode phases of LLM inference?
options: Prefill handles long inputs; decode handles short inputs
Prefill processes all input tokens in parallel (compute-bound); decode generates output tokens one at a time (memory-bandwidth-bound)
Prefill happens on CPU; decode happens on GPU
Prefill uses attention; decode uses the feed-forward network only
mcqs.explanation: Prefill processes all input tokens simultaneously in a single parallel forward pass — it's compute-bound and fast. Decode generates each output token in a separate forward pass, one at a time (autoregressive), and is memory-bandwidth-bound because model weights must be loaded for each token.

mcqs.question: What does TTFT (Time to First Token) measure?
options: The total time to generate a complete response
The time from request submission to when the first output token is produced
The time between consecutive output tokens during streaming
The time taken to load the model into GPU memory
mcqs.explanation: TTFT measures the latency from when a request is sent to when the first output token appears. For interactive applications, TTFT < 500ms is typically needed to feel responsive. It's dominated by prefill time for long prompts.

mcqs.question: Why is the decode phase described as memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound?
options: Decoding uses no GPU and relies entirely on CPU memory
Each decode step generates only one token, requiring a tiny matrix-vector multiply — the GPU finishes quickly but must wait for model weights to load from HBM
The attention computation during decode exceeds the GPU's compute capacity
Decode is limited by the speed of the tokenizer, not the GPU
mcqs.explanation: In decode, each step produces one token (a matrix-vector multiply, not matrix-matrix). The GPU completes this tiny computation almost instantly but must load the full model weights from High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) for each step. The HBM read bandwidth, not compute, is the bottleneck.

mcqs.question: What is the primary inference optimization that vLLM introduces?
options: Quantizing model weights to INT4 to reduce computation
PagedAttention — OS-virtual-memory-inspired KV cache management that eliminates memory fragmentation and enables continuous batching
Using flash attention to reduce the attention computation from O(n²) to O(n)
Replacing the transformer decoder with a more efficient RNN
mcqs.explanation: vLLM's key innovation is PagedAttention, which manages the KV cache in fixed-size pages (like OS page tables). This eliminates the 60–80% memory waste from pre-allocation fragmentation and enables continuous batching, achieving 2–24× higher throughput than naive implementations.

mcqs.question: Why does batching multiple requests together significantly improve throughput in LLM inference?
options: Batching allows the model to skip attention computation for shorter requests
Multiple requests can share the cost of loading model weights from GPU HBM, amortizing the memory-bandwidth bottleneck
Batching enables the model to use lower precision arithmetic
Batching reduces tokenization overhead
mcqs.explanation: Since decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, model weights must be loaded regardless of how many tokens are generated. Processing 8 requests in a single batch loads the weights once but generates 8 tokens simultaneously — 8× the throughput for the same bandwidth cost.

mcqs.question: Why do cloud LLM APIs typically charge more per output token than per input token?
options: Output tokens contain more information than input tokens
Output tokens require separate autoregressive decode steps while input tokens are processed in a single parallel prefill pass
Output token generation requires more powerful GPUs than input processing
The cost reflects the higher computational complexity of the language model head
mcqs.explanation: Input tokens are processed in parallel during prefill (one pass for all N input tokens). Each output token requires a separate sequential forward pass through the entire model during decode. This fundamental asymmetry means output tokens are inherently more expensive to generate than input tokens are to process.

mcqs.question: What effect does INT4 quantization have on LLaMA 3 70B inference compared to FP16?
options: It doubles the memory usage but halves the latency
It reduces model size from ~140 GB to ~35 GB and roughly triples token generation throughput on the same hardware
It has no effect on inference speed, only on training speed
It improves TTFT but makes TBT worse
mcqs.explanation: INT4 quantization reduces model precision from 16 bits to 4 bits per parameter, shrinking LLaMA 3 70B from ~140 GB to ~35 GB. This dramatically reduces the HBM bandwidth required per token, approximately tripling generation throughput. Quality degrades slightly but remains acceptable with modern methods like AWQ.

flashcards.back: The process of running a trained LLM to generate a response. Consists of two phases: prefill (process input tokens in parallel) and decode (generate output tokens one at a time).
Latency from request submission to the first output token appearing. Dominated by prefill time. Critical for perceived responsiveness in interactive applications; target < 500ms for chat.
The time between successive output tokens during the decode phase. Determines streaming text speed. Target < 50ms (> 20 tok/s) for smooth real-time text streaming.

The number of tokens generated per second, usually measured across all concurrent requests. Maximized by batching multiple requests together. Distinct from latency — high throughput may coexist with high per-request latency.

Processing multiple inference requests simultaneously in a single forward pass. The primary throughput optimization for decode: amortizes the memory-bandwidth cost of loading model weights across multiple requests.

A single arithmetic operation on floating-point numbers. LLM training/inference compute is measured in FLOPs or FLOPs/s (hardware throughput). GPT-3 training: 3.14×10²³ FLOPs. A100 GPU: ~312 TFLOP/s (BF16).

The rate at which data can be transferred between GPU High Bandwidth Memory and GPU compute cores. The decode bottleneck: each token requires loading ~billions of model weight values. A100: ~2 TB/s. H100: ~3.35 TB/s.

The maximum tolerable latency for a production application. Interactive chat: < 3s total, TTFT < 500ms. Autocomplete: TTFT < 100ms. Agentic chains multiply per-call latency — each LLM hop adds to the total pipeline latency.

videos.title: LLM Inference Explained — Prefill, Decode, and Throughput
videos.description: Detailed walkthrough of the two-phase LLM inference pipeline, why decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, and how batching and quantization improve throughput. Covers TTFT, TBT, and hardware considerations.

videos.title: vLLM: Easy, Fast, and Cheap LLM Serving
videos.description: Overview of vLLM and PagedAttention — how managing the KV cache like OS virtual memory pages eliminates fragmentation and enables continuous batching for dramatically higher inference throughput.
